title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
text:
In this five-week seminar block, we will build a complete sports analytics workflow in public, using NBA basketball as the shared worked example. By the end of the block, you will have a live dashboard on GitHub Pages and a repository that shows your project process clearly.
This week is about foundations. We are not analysing data yet. We are making sure your tools, folders and version control are in place so that the later analytical work runs smoothly.
We will:

title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
text:
1 Key Terms
Before we start, it helps to separate three terms that sound similar but do different jobs.

Git is the version-control software installed on your computer. It tracks changes to files over time.
GitHub is the website that hosts Git repositories online, so you can back up your work, share it, and submit it publicly.
GitHub Pages is GitHub’s web publishing service. Later in the module, you will use it to publish a website version of your project, such as a dashboard or portfolio page.

In short: Git tracks your work locally, GitHub stores and shares it online, and GitHub Pages turns part of that online repository into a website.

title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
text:
4 Initial Setup

4.1 Step 1: Install the Core Packages
Run the following in the RStudio Console.



R console

install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("hoopR")
install.packages("sportyR")
install.packages("plotly")
install.packages("ggrepel")
install.packages("shiny")
install.packages("shinylive")
install.packages("usethis")
install.packages("gitcreds")


Then verify that the key packages load.



R console

library(tidyverse)
library(hoopR)
library(sportyR)
library(plotly)
library(shiny)








If package installation fails, read the error carefully before retrying. Most problems come from a missing system dependency, a permissions issue, or an interrupted download.





4.2 Step 2: Create the Project
An RStudio Project keeps all of the files for one piece of work together in one folder. The file with the .Rproj extension stores the project settings, and opening that file tells RStudio, “this is the working directory for this project”.
Create a new RStudio project:

File -> New Project -> New Directory -> New Project
project name: nba-analytics
tick Create a git repository
for the location, choose your OneDrive folder
inside OneDrive, create a folder called ULMS744 if you do not already have one
create nba-analytics inside that ULMS744 folder

So the project should live in a location similar to:
OneDrive/
└── ULMS744/
    └── nba-analytics/
This matters because you need a folder structure you can find quickly, back up easily, and reuse throughout the module.
Let’s go through how to open this project together. First, quit your session by going to File -> Close Project. Whenever you want to start your new R session, go to File -> Open Project and then navigate to the folder where you saved your project. Once you open that folder, click the .Rproj file (in our case, you should see a file called nba-analytics.Rproj in the folder). Now we’re ready!






Once you open a project, you should see the file panel update automatically.



Then create the project folders we will use throughout the block (paste below in the RStudio Console).



R console

dir.create("data")
dir.create("scripts")
dir.create("output")
dir.create("app")


Your project should now look like this:
nba-analytics/
├── app/
├── data/
├── output/
├── scripts/
├── nba-analytics.Rproj
└── .gitignore


4.3 Step 3: Create a Simple README
A README.md file is the short front-page description of a project. When someone opens your repository on GitHub, the README is usually the first thing they see. It should explain, in plain language, what the project is and what it is for.
In the Files pane in RStudio:

click New File
choose Text File
save it as README.md
copy and paste the text below into the file

# NBA Analytics Workflow

A seminar project for ULMS744: Sports Analytics in Practice.

This project uses NBA basketball as a worked example to build a reproducible sports analytics workflow in R.

Tools used: tidyverse, hoopR, sportyR, plotly, Shiny, and Shinylive.


4.4 Step 4: Check .gitignore
The .gitignore file tells Git which files it should not track. This is useful for temporary files, machine-specific files, and files that would create clutter or problems if uploaded to GitHub.
In the Files pane in RStudio:

click Open File
choose .gitignore
copy and paste the text below into the file

.Rhistory
.Rdata
.RData
.Ruserdata
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
*.zip
*.gz






What do these entries mean, and why ignore them?

.Rhistory is a record of commands typed in the R console.
.Rdata and .RData are saved R workspace files.
.Ruserdata stores user-specific session settings.
.DS_Store is a hidden macOS system file.
Thumbs.db is a hidden Windows system file.
*.zip and *.gz mean any compressed archive with those endings.

We ignore these files because they are usually temporary, computer-specific, or not part of the main project deliverable. Keeping them out of the repository makes the project cleaner and reduces the chance of uploading unnecessary files to GitHub.





4.5 Step 5: Create the GitHub Repository
GitHub is a website that hosts Git repositories online. It gives you a remote repository, meaning a copy of your project that lives on the web rather than only on your own computer.
A repository or repo is the full project folder that Git tracks over time, including its files and version history.
On GitHub:

create a new repository called nba-analytics [how-to guide];
make it Public;
do not add a README or .gitignore on GitHub.

At this point you will have:

a local repository on your computer inside your RStudio project folder;
a remote repository on GitHub.

The local repository is where you do the work. The remote repository is the online copy that you push to GitHub so it can be backed up, shared, and submitted as part of your portfolio.


4.6 Step 6: Authenticate and Connect
Create a Personal Access Token:



R console

usethis::create_github_token()


This will open GitHub in your browser so that you can create a Personal Access Token (PAT). A PAT is like a password that allows RStudio and Git to talk securely to your GitHub account.
After GitHub shows you the token:

copy the token immediately;
return to RStudio;
run the command below in the Console;
paste the token when R asks for it;
press Enter.




R console

gitcreds::gitcreds_set()


This stores the token securely on your computer so that Git can authenticate when you push to GitHub.
Then open the Terminal tab inside RStudio and add the remote repository, replacing YOUR-USERNAME.






For command-line steps in this workbook, use the Terminal tab inside RStudio.



Terminal tab shown inside the red box.








RStudio Terminal

git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/nba-analytics.git
git branch -M main


This command links your local repository to the remote repository on GitHub. After that, your local project and the online project can talk to each other.


4.7 Step 7: Make Your First Commit
In Git, staging means telling Git, “these are the files I want to include in my next save point”. You can think of the staging area as a waiting area for changes that are ready to be recorded.
A commit is that recorded save point. It is a snapshot of your project at a particular moment, together with a short message explaining what you did.
For this first commit, you want to include the files you have just created for the project setup.
Then commit with the message:
Initial project setup
In simple terms, the workflow is:

stage the files, meaning choose what will go into the snapshot;
commit the files, meaning save that snapshot into the Git history;
push the commit, meaning send that saved snapshot to GitHub.

In the RStudio Terminal, the commands for your first commit are:



RStudio Terminal

git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup"
git push -u origin main


What each command does:

git add . stages all current files in the project folder for the next commit.
git commit -m "Initial project setup" creates the snapshot and gives it a message.
git push -u origin main uploads that commit to GitHub and connects your local main branch to the remote one.

Push to GitHub and verify that the repository is visible online. Once this is done, you have created both the local project history and the remote online copy of that history.



Your new repository should now look like this.

title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
text:
5 Independent Practice
Complete the following:

create scripts/00-setup.R;
add the library() calls for the main packages;
commit and push that file.




scripts/00-setup.R

library(tidyverse)
library(hoopR)
library(sportyR)
library(plotly)
library(shiny)
library(shinylive)

title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
text:
6 Common Issues







Problem
Solution




Git pane not visible
Check Tools -> Global Options -> Git/SVN and confirm Git is installed


fatal: not a git repository
Recreate the project with Create a git repository ticked, or run git init in the project


Push rejected
Check the remote URL and PAT


Package install fails
Restart R and retry; check for operating-system-specific setup issues

title: 1. Foundations of Reproducible Analytics
Week 7 Lecture
text: Module Overview

title: Sports Analytics in Practice
text:
Welcome!
Welcome to the course website dedicated to the ULMS744 module: Sports Analytics in Practice! On this site, you will find the coding seminar materials. You can navigate to each week’s material by clicking on the specific sidebar menu link. Please do let me know if you are struggling to access anything. Below you will find information related to the organisational aspects of the course. These are also covered in the intro lecture slides.
This revised seminar block uses basketball as the common worked example while preparing you to transfer the same workflow to a different sport in the assignment.

title: Sports Analytics in Practice
text:
Pre-requisites
This module builds directly on the ULMS725 module: Introduction to Programming and Analysis, extending the programming and analytical skills introduced there into more applied contexts.
Students are therefore expected to have access to a functioning installation of R (version 4.5+) and to be comfortable with core R workflows. This includes skills such as installing/ using packages, importing/ manipulating datasets and restructuring data for analysis.

title: Sports Analytics in Practice
text:
Key Details
Shunya Kimura will be taking Semester 2 Weeks 07 – 11.
Contact: s.kimura@liverpool.ac.uk (book a meeting)
The module is also supported by Ayan Orujob who will be taking over the first six weeks. He offers his own office hours for additional support outside of the lectures/seminars, and he’ll let you know when they take place.
Keep an eye on the module announcements page for further updates and consult Canvas for all essential module information, including lecture slides/recordings, reading lists and key details about assignments submission etc. This workbook is designed primarily to support the seminar sessions, and it contains the key materials, exercises and guidance that we will work through during seminars.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
Last week, we set up the project and connected it to GitHub. This week we move into the first core analytical task: sourcing and preparing sports data through an API workflow in R.
We will use hoopR to pull NBA basketball data. Basketball remains our common seminar example because it gives us rich public data and a clear route into visualisation and dashboard building. The workflow, however, is transferable: in another sport, the package and variables will change, but the project logic will stay the same.
We will:

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
2 A Publishing Shortcut: _publish.sh
Last week, we committed and pushed work to GitHub using three separate Terminal commands. That works, but typing the same commands every time gets repetitive. Let’s create a small shortcut script that does it all in one step.

2.1 Create the File
In the Files pane in RStudio:

click New File
choose Text File
save it as _publish.sh in your project root (the same level as nba-analytics.Rproj)
paste the following into the file and save




_publish.sh

# _publish.sh — A shortcut to save and publish your work to GitHub.
#
# Usage (in your R terminal):
#   ./_publish.sh "Your commit message here"
#
# Example:
#   ./_publish.sh "Add cleaned NBA team and player data"

git add .
git commit -m "$1"
git push -u origin main

echo "Done! Your work has been published to GitHub."




2.2 How to Use It
From now on, whenever you want to save and publish your work, open the RStudio Terminal and run:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Your commit message here"


Replace the text inside the quotes with a short description of what you did. For example:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Add cleaned NBA team and player data"


That single command will stage all your changes, commit them with your message, and push everything to GitHub. You will use this throughout the rest of the module.






The underscore in _publish.sh is intentional — it keeps the file near the top of your file list and signals that it is a helper script, not part of the main project output.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
3 Why This Matters
Analysts are often judged on whether they can obtain and prepare their own data, not only on whether they can plot a spreadsheet someone else has already cleaned.
The workflow for today is:
documentation -> choose parameters -> pull -> inspect -> summarise -> save
That sequence is one of the key transferable habits from this module.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
4 Initial Setup
Load the libraries.



R code

library(tidyverse)
library(hoopR)

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
5 SportsDataverse Workflow

5.1 Reading the Documentation First
Before calling any data-sourcing function, you should read its documentation. The documentation tells you what data sources are available, what functions to call, what parameters they accept, and what kind of data they return. This saves time and avoids guesswork.

5.1.1 Start with the Package Website
Every SportsDataVerse package has a documentation website. This is the best place to start because it gives you the big picture: what data sources the package can access, what functions are available, and how to get started.
For hoopR, the getting-started guide is here: https://hoopr.sportsdataverse.org/articles/getting-started-hoopR.html
From that page, you can see the main functions the package offers (e.g. load_nba_team_box(), load_nba_player_box()), what data they return, and how to use them. Browse the package website to discover what is available before you start writing code.


5.1.2 Then Check Individual Functions in R
Once you know which function you want to use, check its detailed documentation in RStudio by typing ? followed by the function name:



R console

?load_nba_team_box


This opens the help page. The three sections to focus on are:

Usage — shows the function signature and its default parameter values.
Arguments — lists every parameter, explains what it controls, and tells you what values are valid.
Value — describes what the function returns (usually a data frame with specific columns).



5.1.3 What You Learn from the hoopR Docs
Reading the documentation for load_nba_team_box(), you discover:

It has one key parameter: seasons.
seasons accepts a numeric vector — you can request a single season (e.g. 2024) or multiple seasons (e.g. 2020:2024).
The function returns a data frame where each row is one team’s performance in one game.

Knowing this before you run the code means you can choose sensible parameter values on the first attempt.


5.1.4 Example: A Football Package
The same documentation habit applies to every sports data package. For example, worldfootballR provides football (soccer) data from sources including FBref and Transfermarkt. If you wanted to pull transfer data from Transfermarkt, you would start with the package documentation:
https://jaseziv.github.io/worldfootballR/articles/extract-transfermarkt-data.html
That page shows you which functions access Transfermarkt data, what parameters they require, and what the returned data looks like. Once you have identified the function you need, check its details in R:



R console

?tm_player_market_values


The Arguments section would tell you parameters like:

country_name — e.g. "England" for the English leagues
start_year — the year the season starts, e.g. 2023 for the 2023/24 season
league_url — optionally, a direct Transfermarkt league URL

So a call might look like:



R code

epl_values <- tm_player_market_values(
  country_name = "England",
  start_year   = 2023
)








You do not need to memorise every parameter. You need to know where to look: start with the package website to see what is available, then use ?function_name in R to check the details. The documentation is always your first stop.






5.2 Step 1: Pull Team Box Score Data
load_nba_team_box() downloads team-level box score data from ESPN via the hoopR API wrapper. Each row in the result represents one team’s performance in one game, with columns for points, shooting percentages, rebounds, assists and more.



R code

nba_team_box <- load_nba_team_box(seasons = 2024)








In hoopR, seasons = 2024 means the NBA season that ends in 2024, i.e. the 2023/24 season. This convention is common across SportsDataVerse packages.





5.3 Step 2: Inspect the Raw Data
Never summarise a dataset before checking what it contains.



R code

dim(nba_team_box)


dim() returns the number of rows and columns. This tells you how large the dataset is — for example, 2460 46 would mean 2,460 rows and 46 columns.



R code

names(nba_team_box)


names() lists every column name. Scan through this to find the variables you care about (team name, score, shooting stats, etc.).



R code

glimpse(nba_team_box)


glimpse() is the most informative single inspection command. It shows each column’s name, type (e.g. <chr>, <dbl>) and first few values — all in one view.



R code

head(nba_team_box)


head() shows the first six rows of the data frame, so you can see actual data values in context.






You can also inspect a data frame by clicking its name in the Environment panel (top-right in RStudio). This opens a spreadsheet-style viewer where you can scroll through rows and columns, sort by clicking column headers, and search for values. It is a quick way to get a feel for the data before writing any code.



Look for:

one row per team per game;
the main team identifier;
score and result columns;
the shooting, rebounding and possession-related variables you may want later.



5.4 Step 3: Prepare a Season-Level Team Table
We want one row per team, using regular-season games only. To get there, we need to group the game-level rows by team and then summarise each group into a single row of season averages.






The variables we use below (e.g. team_score, field_goals_made, total_rebounds) come from inspecting the data in the previous step. You will need to look at what columns are available in your data and decide which ones are useful for your summary. Use names() or the Environment viewer to check what is there.






R code

nba_regular <- nba_team_box |>
  filter(season_type == 2)

nba_team_stats <- nba_regular |>
  group_by(team_short_display_name) |>
  summarise(
    games = n(),
    wins = sum(team_winner == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE),
    losses = sum(team_winner == FALSE, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_pts = round(mean(team_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_fg_pct = round(mean(field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_3pt_made = round(mean(three_point_field_goals_made, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_rebounds = round(mean(total_rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_assists = round(mean(assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_turnovers = round(mean(turnovers, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_steals = round(mean(steals, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_blocks = round(mean(blocks, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    win_pct = round(wins / games * 100, 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(win_pct))


A few things to note about the code above:

group_by(team_short_display_name) splits the data into separate groups, one per team. Everything inside summarise() is then calculated within each group rather than across the whole dataset.
summarise() collapses each group into a single row. The columns you create inside it (e.g. games = n(), avg_pts = round(mean(...))) define what the summary table contains.
.groups = "drop" tells R to remove the grouping structure after summarising. Without this, the data frame would remain grouped, which can cause unexpected behaviour in later operations.
mutate() adds or modifies columns without changing the number of rows. Here we use it to calculate win_pct from the wins and games columns that summarise() just created.
avg_fg_pct is calculated from field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100 because the raw data provides made and attempted counts rather than a pre-calculated percentage.

Inspect the cleaned table.



R code

print(nba_team_stats, n = 30)
summary(nba_team_stats)
colSums(is.na(nba_team_stats))




5.5 Step 4: Prepare a Player Table
We also need a player-level table for later charts and dashboard views. The same group_by() |> summarise() pattern applies — this time grouping by player name and team.






Again, the column names below (e.g. points, rebounds, field_goals_made) come from inspecting the player box score data. Run names(nba_player_box) or click the data frame in the Environment panel to see what columns are available, and choose the ones that are relevant to your analysis.






R code

nba_player_box <- load_nba_player_box(seasons = 2024)

nba_player_stats <- nba_player_box |>
  filter(season_type == 2, minutes > 0) |>
  group_by(athlete_display_name, team_short_display_name) |>
  summarise(
    games = n(),
    avg_pts = round(mean(points, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_rebounds = round(mean(rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_assists = round(mean(assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_fg_pct = round(mean(field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_3pt_made = round(mean(three_point_field_goals_made, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_minutes = round(mean(minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(games >= 40) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_pts))





R code

head(nba_player_stats, 20)




5.6 Step 5: Save Reusable Data Files

5.6.1 What Is an .rds File?
An .rds file is R’s native format for saving a single object to disk. It preserves the R object exactly — column types, factor levels, attributes and all. When you load it back with readRDS(), you get the same object you saved.
We also save .csv versions because CSV is a universal format that can be opened in Excel, Python, or any other tool. Use .rds for your own R workflow; use .csv when sharing with others.



R code

saveRDS(nba_team_stats, "data/nba_2024_team_stats.rds")
write_csv(nba_team_stats, "data/nba_2024_team_stats.csv")

saveRDS(nba_player_stats, "data/nba_2024_player_stats.rds")
write_csv(nba_player_stats, "data/nba_2024_player_stats.csv")


Check that the files exist.



R code

list.files("data")








Saving prepared data is part of the workflow, not a tidying afterthought. It means you do not need to re-run long API calls every time you want to make a chart.

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
6 Independent Practice: Your Own Sport
The basketball walkthrough above is the shared seminar example, but your individual assignment will ask you to source, clean and present data for a sport of your own choosing. This is a good opportunity to get a head start.

6.1 What to Do

Pick a sport and a SportsDataVerse package (or another R package that provides sports data through an API). Some options:



Sport
Package
Documentation




WNBA / women’s college basketball
wehoop
https://wehoop.sportsdataverse.org/


NFL (American football)
nflreadr
https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/


MLB (baseball)
baseballr
https://billpetti.github.io/baseballr/


Football (soccer)
worldfootballR
https://jaseziv.github.io/worldfootballR/


F1 (motorsport)
f1dataR
https://scasanova.github.io/f1dataR/



Install the package if you have not already, and load it.
Read the documentation for the main data-loading function in that package. Use ?function_name in the Console or browse the package website. Identify:

what parameters the function requires;
what values are valid for each parameter;
what kind of data frame it returns.

Pull one season of data using the function and your chosen parameters.
Inspect the result using dim(), names(), glimpse() and head().
Summarise the data into a clean table with one row per team (or per player, driver, etc.), keeping the variables you find most interesting.
Save both .rds and .csv versions to your data/ folder.







The individual assignment will ask you to do exactly this kind of work — source data from an API, prepare it, and build outputs from it. The more you practise now, the less you will need to figure out later. Think of this exercise as the first step of your assignment.





6.2 Commit and Push
Once you are happy with your work, use your _publish.sh shortcut:



RStudio Terminal

./_publish.sh "Add data for [your sport]"

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
7 Transfer Checkpoint
The details are basketball-specific, but the project habit is not. For another sport, the equivalent questions would be:

what is the raw event or match-level table?
what should one cleaned team-level row represent?
what should one cleaned player, driver, batter or club row represent?
which variables need to be saved for charting and dashboards later?

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
text:
8 Common Issues







Problem
Solution




hoopR call fails
Check your internet connection and retry


Returned data frame is very wide
Use glimpse() and select() to focus on relevant variables


Column names differ slightly
Check names() and adapt the code


Missing values appear
Use na.rm = TRUE and inspect where they occur


Package not found
Run install.packages("package_name") first


Documentation page is empty
Try the package website instead of ? — some packages document online only

title: 2. API & Data Sourcing
